title: Cases - HampdenV.Walsh
content:
(1 Queen's Bench Division, 189)

        "Five hundred pounds as stake I'll lay,"
        Says Hampden, "that by such a day
        No man of science proves to me
        That earth not flat but round must be;
        The earth is flat, and flats are they."
        The sum Walsh holds right willingly;
        But Wallace by philosophy
        Proves roundness, and would take away
                        Five hundred pounds.

        "Proof me no proofs," quoth Hampden, "Nay,
        Let Wallace get it if he may,
        I'll sue Walsh for it." So sues he.
        "Let Wallace," hold the judges three,
        "Take nought, let Walsh to Hampden pay
                        Five hundred pounds."
meaning:
This evocative piece by James Williams represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of James Williams's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
